# Transcrição: Lesson 14- Product Creation Foundation

- **Fonte:** Lesson 14- Product Creation Foundation
- **Duração:** 11:30
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:00]

So let's look at some of the real foundational things here. First of all, you, when you go to create a product, I know this sounds funny, but a lot of people do this, they don't think about how they're actually going to use the product. They just get an idea and it seems like a good one. So you really need to get clear on this. Are you just doing this product to make money? And when I say that, I don't want any negative associations with that, you know, when I say just doing it to make money. I mean, generally, if this is your first product or one of your first products, that's what you do the product for is to make money. And that's awesome. I mean, that's what business is all about. That's what we're talking about. And that's probably why you originally got this course, which you decided you wanted to make some money, you wanted to have your own product. That's fantastic. So, but you know, there's lots of other reasons you could do it, or there's reasons, other things you'll get out of it. One might be to build a list of clients. Like you could do the first product and it's simply, you know, you've got a bigger picture to create a bigger product down the road. And you're just doing this first product to get people to buy from you and to create this list of clients. Because you know that selling to those clients in the future will, you know, will be much easier if you've sold them that first product. So another thing you could do is maybe it's just to drive traffic. And that could be tied to having partners. So like if you're in a market that's got lots of great affiliates, how do you motivate those affiliates to send traffic to your site by creating a product where they can make a lot of money? You might have a different end goal, but you know, for your website, for your web presence, for your business, and you need traffic. So you create a product. Maybe it's a lower end product and maybe you give 70 or 80 or 100% commission and that means you have tons of affiliates driving traffic to your site. So maybe you create a product and it's not just the end goals and to make money, but it's to create this web presence, to create this site that can slas a traffic. And you're using that product as a tool to generate money for partners, for affiliates. Maybe, you know, in doing that, you would build a list of partners. And that's the way to start building a long term business. If you can start building a big list of partners that are driving a lot of traffic to your site, now you can start building a list, whether it's an opt-in list of prospects or clients. You can see how you can use a product to do a lot of different things. Another thing you can do is you can build positioning. A lot of times people will create a book or report or an e-book and get it out there and we'll establish them as the expert in a given area. And that will lead to things like, you know, high-end consulting or high-end coaching or, you know, who knows, a TV show or a radio show. It doesn't matter. You can build positioning by having a product. And remember, there's often a lot of overlap among these areas.

## [03:00 - 06:01]

You know, often when you start creating products, it's amazing all the different extra benefits that will come to you in addition to making money. One thing, you know, you'll see me hit this over and over. I just talked about it a little bit up here and I've talked about it already in other videos in the course and I'm going to be talking about it more. But you always have to make sure that the product is a product that your market wants. So does the market want your product? What are the people saying in the forums? Are advertisers, you know, go Google it and see if people are already advertising. And when I say Google it, that would be your key phrase. Whatever your key phrase is, you would Google. Do you have competition? Have you run a survey? If you have already have a business and you've got an email list or you've got traffic coming into your site, you can run a survey and you can ask people whether they want the product. You can judge their interest. What's your price point? So this can be a really tough one. The answer always is to far as far as what's the best price is to test the price. But you always, I know you're sitting there thinking, okay, well, I'm the product made. I don't have traffic. How do I test the thing? And also price testing can get a little funny at times. So when you're thinking about your price point, first of all, I should probably take this and put this up here. You don't need to finalize the pricing right now, but it's good to have a general idea. If you're just, I mean, are you creating a low end offer, you know, a sort of an entry level like an ebook? Are you creating this home study course that you're going to ship out in books and DVDs? Are you creating a membership site? Are you creating a high end coaching program, maybe a consulting practice? But some of the things you need to look at is, you know, first of all, what's the value of your product? What kind of results will it get for people? And what will the market pay for your product? In the end, the market will determine what the value is and what it should be priced at. You can also look at what competitor prices are. And generally, I mentioned this, if I'm looking at a market, you know, usually there's a wide range. I mean, if you're looking at cars, you can spend, you know, you know, at the very low end, I don't know what the current low end cars are. It used to be the Ugo. I don't think they make those anymore. But you can look at the real low end and then you can look at, you know, Lamborghinis or Bentley's or whatever at the top. And there's a huge range. I mean, the cars at the top cost, you know, probably 10 to 30 times more than the cars at the bottom of the pricing range. And they'll, you know, if you're going to the grocery store, they're going to do the same thing. They're going to get you to the grocery store. You know, they might get you there in different degrees of style, in different degrees of speed, in different degrees of comfort, in different degrees of safety.

## [06:01 - 09:01]

But they're all going to get you there, right? And it's going to be the same way in your market. You can be at the low end or you can be at the high end. Generally, when you're selling information products, you know, when you create more value, that allows you to be at the high end. Also, when you have better marketing, it allows you to be at the high end. When you have better customer service. But you have to decide where you're going to be. And, you know, generally, I like to be at the higher end of the market. It's just a, it's a better place to be. It's a better business to have. I've seen in the insides of a lot of businesses. And it's rare where I've seen someone serving the low end of the market where I'm like, wow, that's a cool business. I wish I had that business. You know, it's the people at the high end. They're generally making more money that have better problems in their business that are dealing with better people in their business. That attract better employees or in their business. It's just generally it's a better business when you're at the higher end. And that's where I like to be. So then the question is, how do you get there? You have to create more value. You have to have a better product. You have to have a better customer experience, better marketing. But it's, it's possible to get there. And that's usually where I'm going to be. So if you're going to look at competitive prices, don't think about like trying to undercut people. Another thing that might come into play is your production cost. You know, what does it cost you to produce the product? You know, generally with information products, it's not going to cost you much at all. You know, downloadable products cost you almost nothing. Your incremental cost for a downloadable product is basically zero. But, you know, there's costs to create in the product. If it's a, if it's a coaching program where you have one on one time, or if it's a live event where you've got a lot of costs, you might have to figure out what your production costs are and is it worth it to you. With these, you know, sort of my business has gotten so successful that I really can't afford to take on products or projects that don't make me a lot of money. I remember years ago when I sort of hit this level where, like, I remember saying this, and this is, gosh, 10 years ago, I'm like, if, I'm looking at a, you know, I started to get into deal flow and have lots and deal flows where you got lots and lots of potential products coming your way. And I'm going to go ahead and put that in here because this is an important point, is deal flow. So once you become successful in your business, once you start to achieve some success, you start to have a lot more deal flow. That means lots more potential deals are coming to you. A lot of people will want to, you know, want you to partner with them. They'll want you to be their expert. They'll want to do interviews with you. They'll want to sell your products. They'll want you to sell their products. They'll want to collaborate and create a product together. Lots and lots of deal flow. And I remember it was probably good 10 or 11 years ago when I'm like, holy cow, my business is so successful that I can't even deal with, I can't even take on a deal unless it's worth at least $50,000 to me. I have to see at least $50,000 or else I can't even get involved in the deal because there's so many great deals coming to me.

## [09:01 - 11:30]

And then I had to gradually ratchet that up. And now, unless some deal's worth like a million or $2 million net to me, it's hard for me to get excited about the deal. There's just so many deals. So here, that might not be part of your production costs or even your profit margin, but it could be one of your criteria. It's like, am I willing to do the work? I mean, a lot of people want to consult with me one-on-one. And I just, it's almost hard for me to charge. The price I would have to charge to do a one-on-one consultation is just off the charts because my business is so successful. And if I took that time and put it in other places in my business, I would, you know, make a lot more money and build an asset in my business. So a lot of this comes into play and it will evolve over time. Just like up in here, why are you doing the product? Probably in the beginning it's to make money, but down the road, it might be more for these things than for making money. And it's the same way here. You know, your standards and your decision criteria will change over time as your deal flow improves or increases. Go and type your key phrases in for all of these and look at the products that are out there and the discussions that are out there and that will help you start to, you know, come up with more and more ideas for products. And just briefly, tools. What you're looking at here is a mind map. So in my mind mapping process, I find pretty useful whenever I am creating products or creating, you know, outlining products. So basically this is, all of this is just a, it's more of a free form outline or a graphical outline. It's a fancier outline. You can use MindJet, MindManager. There's lots of these programs, FreeMind. Just Google that and you'll find it. Bubble.us is an online tool that will allow you to do this type of thing. And a lot of times, I'll actually do this on paper, with paper and pencil in my Idea Journal. And a lot of times I'd rather do that. I just like the actual physical writing, the paper and pencil and the, and drawing connections between things. I love to do that. So you don't have to get fancy with this process at all.
